sections.heading: RT
sections.content:
Radiation therapy plays multiple roles in bladder cancer management: (1) Bladder preservation with concurrent chemoradiotherapy as a category 1 alternative to radical cystectomy for MIBC stages II and IIIA in select patients; (2) Adjuvant RT (category 2B) for patients with pT3-4 disease with positive nodes or margins post-cystectomy; (3) Palliative RT for metastatic disease or recurrent pelvic tumor; (4) Definitive RT for urethral carcinoma. Radiotherapy alone is inferior to chemoradiotherapy and is not considered standard for patients who can tolerate combined therapy.

Principles:
- Precede RT alone or concurrent chemoradiotherapy by visibly complete TUR of the tumor when safely possible [BL-H]
- Simulating and treating patients with empty bladder preferred for daily reproducibility; bladder full for tumor boosts acceptable with image guidance [BL-H]
- Use multiple fields from high-energy linear accelerator beams [BL-H]
- Concurrent chemoradiotherapy is recommended for added tumor cytotoxicity and can be given without significantly increased toxicity over RT alone [BL-H,231]
- Concurrent Fluorouracil and Mitomycin C or low-dose Gemcitabine can be used instead of cisplatin-containing regimens in patients with low or moderate renal function [BL-H]
- Bladder preservation with concurrent chemoradiotherapy is most successful for patients without moderate/severe hydronephrosis and without extensive CIS associated with their muscle-invading tumor [BL-H,ff]
- Optimal candidates for bladder preservation: solitary tumors, negative nodes, no extensive/multifocal CIS, no tumor-related moderate/severe hydronephrosis, good pre-treatment bladder function, <6 cm tumor, allows visually complete or maximally debulking TURBT [ff]

Dose Frameworks:
1. Whole bladder RT with or without pelvic nodal RT: 39.6-50.4 Gy at 1.8-2.0 Gy/fraction, 22-28 fractions, conventional or accelerated hyperfractionation. Initial treatment for bladder preservation; elective nodal treatment optional.
2. Bladder tumor boost: 60-66 Gy at 1.8-2.0 Gy/fraction, 33-37 fractions, sequential to whole bladder RT. Boost to whole or partial bladder for invasive disease.
3. Hypofractionated bladder RT: 55 Gy at 2.75 Gy/fraction, 20 fractions over 4 weeks. Non-inferior to 64 Gy in 32 fractions; superior for invasive local control [232].
4. Alternative hypofractionation: 55 Gy at 2.75 Gy/fraction, 20 fractions over 4 weeks. Reasonable alternative to conventional fractionation [BL-H].
5. Adjuvant pelvic RT post-cystectomy: 45-50.4 Gy at 1.8-2.0 Gy/fraction, 25-28 fractions, conventional fractionation. For pT3/pT4 urothelial bladder cancer following radical cystectomy with ileal conduit (category 2B).
6. Boost for positive margins/extranodal extension post-cystectomy: 54-60 Gy at 1.8-2.0 Gy/fraction, 30-33 fractions, sequential boost. Boost for involved resection margins and areas of extranodal extension if feasible based on normal tissue constraints.
7. Palliative bladder RT: 30 Gy at 3 Gy/fraction, 10 fractions over 2 weeks. Palliative RT for metastatic or recurrent disease.
8. Palliative hypofractionated RT: 21 Gy at 7 Gy/fraction, 3 fractions over 1-2 weeks. Alternative palliative regimen [BL-H].

Approaches:
- Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with 5-FU/Mitomycin C: 5-FU + Mitomycin C concurrent with RT (category 1 for bladder preservation). 5-FU continuous infusion + Mitomycin C bolus. Preferred radiosensitizer for bladder preservation; category 1 evidence from BC2001 trial: 2-year locoregional DFS 67% vs 54% with RT alone (P=.01); 5-year OS 48% vs 35% (P=.16) [37,231]. Key trial: BC2001 (James ND, et al. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1477-1488). Toxicities: Low rates of late grade ≥3 toxicities: 5.7% genitourinary, 1.9% gastrointestinal; no late grade 4 toxicities or treatment-related deaths [230].
- Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with Cisplatin alone: Cisplatin concurrent with RT (preferred regimen). Cisplatin weekly. Preferred radiosensitizer for bladder preservation. Key trial: Multiple RTOG trials [35,36]. Toxicities: Myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity.
- Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with low-dose Gemcitabine: Low-dose gemcitabine concurrent with RT (preferre
